acceptance_targets: [ch02-craft-01] In the revised lines 49-99, at least one of these is verifiable by reading the passage: (1) Nora's narration contains a sentence where she registers the precision of Caleb's conversational aim — using language that reads as 'he really gets me' on first read and 'he was designed to get me' on re-read; OR (2) one exchange includes a beat of near-miss or micro-imperfection that distinguishes organic chemistry from frictionless calibration, resolved within one to two lines. The disturbance must not exceed two sentences and must not make Nora actively doubt Caleb — it should feel like noticing and dismissing, not investigating.

[ch02-craft-02] Nora's interior narration in the revised chapter contains at least one instance of natural profanity ('shit' or 'fuck') in the bad-profiles section (lines 9-16) or the bath-swiping sequence (line 17). The profanity should be organic inner commentary, verifiable by reading the passage and confirming it sounds like how the character already thinks, with added edge rather than imposed edginess.

[ch02-craft-03] The revised final paragraph (line 175) ends on a specific, concrete detail — a line of dialogue, a physical sensation, an observed particular, or an action — rather than on a summary characterization of how the morning felt. The words 'in a way that' and 'the particular feeling' do not appear in the revised final paragraph. The last sentence should be verifiably grounded in the physical or conversational world of the scene.

[MED-01-editing-metaphor-ch02] Within Ch2 (lines 254-430): (1) At most 3 editing/publishing metaphors remain (the 2 literal job references plus 1 organic figurative use). (2) Lines 258 and 266 use non-editorial metaphor domains. (3) The replacement metaphors are specific and sensory, not generic. FAIL if more than 3 editing metaphors remain or if replacements are abstract.

[MED-10-formula-metaphor-ch02] Within Ch2 (lines 254-430): (1) The 'arrived with a conscientiousness' construction at line 266 no longer uses the arrival/remind template. (2) At most 1 instance of the '[X] arrived/landed/sat like [Y]' formula remains. FAIL if both instances remain or if the replacement uses the same syntactic template.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised lines 49-99, at least one of these is verifiable by reading the passage: (1) Nora's narration contains a sentence where she registers the precision of Caleb's conversational aim — using language that reads as 'he really gets me' on first read and 'he was designed to get me' on re-read; OR (2) one exchange includes a beat of near-miss or micro-imperfection that distinguishes organic chemistry from frictionless calibration, resolved within one to two lines. The disturbance must not exceed two sentences and must not make Nora actively doubt Caleb — it should feel like noticing and dismissing, not investigating.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:49-99 — The entire Nora-Caleb conversation. Caleb reads The Understory, a book Nora edited (line 29), asks the exact right question about manuscript reading (line 63: 'What do you look for when a manuscript first lands on your desk?'), gives a precisely calibrated response to Nora's novel confession (line 79: 'Three years is a long time to keep something alive...'), mirrors her sentence rhythms and metaphor style throughout, and produces zero mismatches across a two-hour exchange. The prose registers all of this as wonderful. At no point does Nora's narration register even a suppressed flicker of 'this is almost too good.' Compare line 65, where Nora notes 'Nobody had asked her that' — she marvels at the quality of the question but does not notice the precision of the aim.
findings.problem: The chapter spine requires 'First conversation crackles with chemistry that is slightly too perfect.' The conversation crackles with chemistry, but the 'slightly too perfect' quality is not encoded in the narrative. Caleb has zero blind spots, zero conversational misses, zero moments where the connection briefly wobbles and recovers — qualities any real first conversation includes, however extraordinary. The result reads as romantic wish fulfillment rather than an extraordinary connection with a hairline crack the reader can later identify as the seam in the illusion. The novel's central irony depends on the reader being able to revisit this conversation and see the engineering beneath the charm. Currently the engineering is invisible because there is no contrast against which to see it. The state delta 'the uncanny perfection of Caleb's profile and conversation' specified in the boundary context is delivered as perfection but not as uncanny.
findings.rewrite_direction: In the conversation at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:49-99, introduce one or two micro-disturbances that Nora notices but dismisses. Target spans: (a) At or near line 65, where Nora notes 'Nobody had asked her that' — add a half-sentence of interior narration where she registers the uncanny precision of the question, a faintest pulse of 'how did he know to ask that?' before the pleasure of being asked overwhelms the flicker. (b) At or near line 79, where Caleb responds to the novel confession — let Nora's narration note, even in passing, that his emotional intelligence feels almost impossible for a first conversation with a stranger, before she attributes it to genuine connection. The disturbance should be quiet enough that a first-time reader absorbs it as romantic excitement but legible enough that a re-reader sees it as evidence of calibration. Do not make Nora suspicious; make the prose suspicious on her behalf.

findings.acceptance_test: Nora's interior narration in the revised chapter contains at least one instance of natural profanity ('shit' or 'fuck') in the bad-profiles section (lines 9-16) or the bath-swiping sequence (line 17). The profanity should be organic inner commentary, verifiable by reading the passage and confirming it sounds like how the character already thinks, with added edge rather than imposed edginess.
findings.evidence: Full chapter: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:1-176. In 176 lines of close-third narration from Nora's POV, she produces zero instances of profanity — not in her internal reaction to bad profiles (lines 9-16), not during frustrated bath-swiping (line 17), not in her private reaction to the man who pitched his detective novel (line 15), not anywhere. Simone uses 'fuck' naturally in a text (line 137: 'who the fuck are you'), establishing that the chapter's register permits profanity. But Nora's internal voice is entirely clean across every register the chapter visits: exasperation, frustration, surprise, private reflection.
findings.problem: The style bible establishes Nora's profanity_profile as 'Moderate. Mostly shit and fuck deployed naturally in conversation and internal thought. Not a heavy swearer but not prudish. Uses profanity more when genuinely angry or caught off-guard, less when performing socially.' Zero profanity across 176 lines of private narration — including scenes of frustration (bad profiles), mild exasperation (the Todd-or-Troy novel pitch), and unguarded interiority (alone in the bath, alone in bed) — is a voice inconsistency. The chapter's comic, hopeful tone does not require heavy profanity, but total absence from a moderate swearer in her most private, unperforming moments is subtle sanitization of the character's established register.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:9-17 (the bad-profiles montage and/or the bath-swiping sequence), introduce one or two instances of natural profanity in Nora's interior narration. Target moments: (a) line 11 or 15, where Nora encounters the specialist profiles or the novel-pitching man — her internal reaction could include a 'shit' or mild 'what the fuck' as wry commentary; (b) line 17, the Friday bath-swiping sequence, where 'a new low' could be accompanied by a private profanity that feels like honest self-assessment. The profanity should emerge from the wry, observational voice already present — it should feel like a natural texture of her thinking, not an insertion.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised final paragraph (line 175) ends on a specific, concrete detail — a line of dialogue, a physical sensation, an observed particular, or an action — rather than on a summary characterization of how the morning felt. The words 'in a way that' and 'the particular feeling' do not appear in the revised final paragraph. The last sentence should be verifiably grounded in the physical or conversational world of the scene.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:171-175. The chapter's final paragraph: 'She took her coffee to the couch and sat cross-legged and began to type, and the morning opened up in front of her in a way that mornings hadn't in a long time — unhurried, full of a quiet, electric attention, the particular feeling of a conversation that was just getting started and could go anywhere and already felt, against every piece of evidence she had about the reliability of dating apps and strangers and hope, like it was going somewhere good.'
findings.problem: The chapter closes on a narrative summary of Nora's emotional state rather than on an embodied moment. The final paragraph tells us the morning 'opened up' and catalogs what that opening felt like ('unhurried, full of a quiet, electric attention, the particular feeling of a conversation...') rather than ending on a specific detail from the conversation, a physical gesture, or a concrete sensory moment. After a chapter that earns its emotion through specific detail — the sloshed bathwater, the sock on its way to the hamper, the baseboard heater ticking, the dishwasher down the hall — the ending retreats into abstraction. The paragraph also contains two of the chapter's identified prose tics ('in a way that mornings hadn't,' 'the particular feeling of').
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:175, close the chapter on a concrete detail rather than a summary of feeling. Options: (a) end on a specific line or exchange from the continuing Caleb conversation — something small and particular that carries the warmth without naming it; (b) end on a physical detail (the coffee cooling untouched, the October light moving across the floor, the phone warm in her hand) that embodies the state change; (c) end on Nora doing something that shows the shift — typing and deleting and retyping with a smile, or reading his message twice. The closing should trust the reader to feel what the chapter has earned through 174 lines of specific observation.

findings.acceptance_test: Within Ch2 (lines 254-430): (1) At most 3 editing/publishing metaphors remain (the 2 literal job references plus 1 organic figurative use). (2) Lines 258 and 266 use non-editorial metaphor domains. (3) The replacement metaphors are specific and sensory, not generic. FAIL if more than 3 editing metaphors remain or if replacements are abstract.
findings.problem: Editing/publishing metaphors appear 5 times in Ch2: 'approach the app the way she approached a manuscript' (258), 'conscientiousness that reminded her of a competent first draft' (266), Caleb asks about manuscripts (316), 'I'm an editor. There's always a record' (344), 'the way the first sentence of a good book sometimes sat in her chest' (346). This metaphor domain appears in 14+ chapters across the novel and is the default register for all emotional processing. While Nora's editorial eye is character-organic, when it becomes the only lens for romantic feeling, grief, desire, friendship, and self-assessment, it stops being character voice and becomes authorial tic. Lines 316 and 344 are organic (Nora literally discussing her job); lines 258, 266, and 346 are ornamental. This chapter should REPLACE at least 2 of the 3 ornamental instances with non-editorial metaphor domains — body, architecture, weather, music, cooking, or other registers available to a woman who lives in Brooklyn and has a rich sensory life.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:258, replace 'the way she approached a manuscript she didn't love on first read' with a non-editorial simile for approaching something with cautious openness. At line 266, replace 'a competent first draft' with a different register for describing Caleb's early messages as promising but not yet fully formed. KEEP line 346 ('the way the first sentence of a good book sat in her chest') as the chapter's strongest editorial metaphor — it earns its place because it's specific and emotionally true. KEEP lines 316 and 344 as literal job discussion.

findings.acceptance_test: Within Ch2 (lines 254-430): (1) The 'arrived with a conscientiousness' construction at line 266 no longer uses the arrival/remind template. (2) At most 1 instance of the '[X] arrived/landed/sat like [Y]' formula remains. FAIL if both instances remain or if the replacement uses the same syntactic template.
findings.problem: The construction '[X] arrived/landed like [Y]' or '[X] sat in [body part] the way [Y]' appears 10+ times across the novel, forming a default simile template. Ch2 has 2 instances: 'messages arrived with a conscientiousness that reminded her of a competent first draft' (266) and 'something that sat in her chest the way the first sentence of a good book sometimes sat in her chest' (346). The first is a mild variant; the second is a clear instance of the formula. The construction becomes visible as pattern by mid-novel. This chapter should REPLACE at least 1 of its 2 instances.
findings.rewrite_direction: KEEP line 346 ('sat in her chest the way the first sentence of a good book sometimes sat in her chest') as a strong instance that combines the formula with the editing metaphor domain to create genuine character texture. REPLACE line 266: instead of 'arrived with a conscientiousness that reminded her of,' use a construction that doesn't rely on the arrival/landing template — describe the messages' quality through a different syntactic structure (direct characterization, a clause of effect, a comparison that doesn't use 'arrived' or 'reminded her of').

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
